audits.name: Stripe
audits.h1: Financial infrastructure to grow your revenue.
audits.headlineVerdict: Names the buyer and the outcome. Modest and confident.

audits.name: Linear
audits.h1: Linear is a purpose-built tool for planning and building products.
audits.headlineVerdict: Names the buyer (product teams) and the action. Honest, not aspirational.

audits.name: Vercel
audits.h1: Develop. Preview. Ship. The frontend cloud.
audits.headlineVerdict: Trio of verbs names the workflow.

audits.name: Notion
audits.h1: Write, plan, share. With AI at your side.
audits.headlineVerdict: Three actions in 6 words. Reads like the product experience.

audits.name: Figma
audits.h1: Where teams bring their next ideas to life
audits.headlineVerdict: Poetic but vague. Names no specific buyer, no specific outcome.

audits.name: Webflow
audits.h1: Build your business on a platform that grows with you
audits.topIssue: Headline is too long to scan
audits.topIssueFix: Webflow serves 4 distinct audiences. Lead with the outcome for one of them.
audits.headlineVerdict: Tries to speak to all four audiences at once.

audits.name: Asana
audits.topIssue: Headline is too short to be specific
audits.topIssueFix: Three words is too short to name a buyer or outcome.
audits.headlineVerdict: Aspirational three-word slogan, not a headline.

audits.name: Calendly
audits.topIssue: Generic page lacks a specific audience
audits.topIssueFix: Three very different personas visit. Lead with a role-specific angle.
audits.headlineVerdict: Names the feature but the verb (easily) is empty filler.

audits.name: Loom
audits.headlineVerdict: Defines a category (async video) rather than selling a feature.

audits.name: Ahrefs
audits.h1: Tools for SEO, content, and marketing
audits.topIssue: Headline reads as a product category, not a promise
audits.topIssueFix: Tools for X describes a category on Amazon. Lead with the outcome.
audits.headlineVerdict: Three nouns in a row read like a feature list.

audits.name: Buffer
audits.h1: Grow your audience and build a brand
audits.topIssue: Page has multiple CTAs splitting attention
audits.topIssueFix: Two CTAs both look like primary buttons. Pick one and demote the other.
audits.headlineVerdict: Names the outcome. Honest but generic.

audits.name: Intercom
audits.headlineVerdict: Three words. Names a category, signals the AI era.

audits.name: Zapier
audits.h1: Automation that is easy and powerful
audits.headlineVerdict: Names the feature plus the value.

audits.name: Airtable
audits.h1: The fastest way to build apps
audits.headlineVerdict: Names the buyer plus the action plus the qualifier.

audits.name: Slack
audits.topIssue: Page has 3 primary CTAs splitting attention
audits.topIssueFix: Three different primary buttons all look like the main CTA. Pick one.
audits.headlineVerdict: Two words. Defines a category rather than a promise.

llmInsights: The median B2B SaaS landing page scores 82, firmly in the Solid band. The top quartile reaches 87, well into Strong. These are well-funded teams with professional copywriters.

Pages that score below 75 share two patterns: vague headlines (Figma, Slack, Asana rely on brand recognition rather than specific promises) and choice-paralysis from multiple primary CTAs (Buffer, Slack).

Pages that score 85+ share a counter-intuitive pattern: a single primary CTA, not three. Loom, Linear, Airtable, Intercom, Vercel each have one button. Visitors do not need options; they need clarity.
The Tools for X headline (Ahrefs, Buffer) reads as a feature list. The trio-of-verbs headline (Vercel, Notion) reads as a workflow. The former is Amazon; the latter is the homepage.
FAQ presence is universal across all 15 pages. What is not table stakes: specific customer counts (10,000+ teams) vs. vague customer categories (leading brands).
Pricing is mentioned on 15/15 pages. Where they differ: 11/15 hide the actual number behind a button, 4/15 show tiers. The show-tiers pages score on average 6 points higher.
llmHeadlinePatterns: Top scorers name a specific outcome in 5-12 words. Bottom scorers reach for poetic 2-3 word slogans that confuse with their brand tagline.
Verbs beat nouns. Move work forward is weaker than Develop. Preview. Ship. Both have rhythm, but only one names what you do.
Three winning patterns emerged: (1) trio-of-verbs (Vercel, Notion), (2) super-category plus qualifier (Loom, Intercom, Linear), (3) one-sentence promise with a number (Airtable, Stripe).
Bottom scorers rely on brand recognition. Top scorers do not need to, the headline sells a product, not a logo.
